channel.title: The Economist - World in Brief
channel.description: Catch up quickly on the global stories that matter

item.title: World in Brief - March 01, 2026
item.description:
President Donald Trump plans to speak with Iran’s new leadership, according to comments he gave to the Atlantic magazine. “They want to talk,” he said, though declined to say when a conversation would happen. An interim three-person panel, formed on Sunday, has replaced Ali Khamenei, the supreme leader killed by American and Israeli strikes. The “leadership council” consists of Masoud Pezeshkian, the president; Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Ejei, the head of the judiciary; and Alireza Arafi, a high-ranking cleric.

America’s armed forces said three service members have been killed and five seriously wounded. They did not say where or how, but denied Iranian reports that missiles launched at the USS Abraham Lincoln hit the aircraft-carrier, saying the strikes did not “even come close”. Mr Trump said American forces had sunk nine Iranian navy ships and “largely destroyed” Iran’s naval headquarters.

Iran continued its retaliatory strikes on other countries in the region. They fired missiles and drones across Israel and at Bahrain, Kuwait and Qatar, where America has military bases. Most were intercepted. But one hit a bomb shelter near Jerusalem, killing at least nine people. Israel, meanwhile, launched more air strikes targeting “the heart” of Tehran, Iran’s capital.

Eight oil-producing countries—including Russia, Saudi Arabia and several Gulf States—announced that they will increase oil output by 206,000 barrels a day in April. The Organisation of the Petroleum Producing Countries and its partners have not boosted production since December. But analysts are sceptical that the proposed hike will be enough to offset the disruption to global oil flows caused by the conflict.

Hundreds of thousands of air passengers were stranded as carriers cancelled flights across the Middle East. Connecting airports, including in Abu Dhabi, Dubai and Doha, grounded all services. Airlines also diverted planes from the region. Meanwhile Iran’s navy is reportedly telling ships they are not allowed to use the Strait of Hormuz, a waterway through which a third of all seaborne oil travels.

In other news: The conflict between Afghanistan and Pakistan showed little sign of cooling, as Afghanistan said it had fired at Pakistani jets. Ukrainian officials suggested that Russia had agreed to American proposals for post-war security guarantees. In his first letter to shareholders, Greg Abel, Berkshire Hathaway’s newish boss, promised to follow a similar strategy to Warren Buffett, his legendary predecessor at the conglomerate.

What will America and Israel strike next? 

The range of targets that America and Israel have hit since they began attacking Iran on Saturday has been wide. But the most important bomb was the one that killed Ali Khamenei, Iran’s supreme leader. After his death, the list of targets is likely to change. In the coming hours and days America and Israel will go through a process known as “battle-damage assessment” to work out what they have destroyed, whom they have killed and which sites might need follow-up bombing.

Both countries will try to hit Iranian missile-launchers as they emerge from hidden positions, with the aim of pre-empting salvoes. If Iran succeeds in killing American personnel at military bases in the Persian Gulf, Donald Trump could intensify his campaign in response. Despite the uncertainty at the top of the Islamic regime, many days of war may still lie ahead.

The threat to Gulf states

Iran wasted little time before lashing out at its Arab neighbours on Saturday. It fired missiles and drones at military bases in Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar, Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates, all of which host American troops. Most of the attacks were swatted away by air defences. It caused more damage to civilian targets: drones hit Kuwait’s international airport and a residential tower in Bahrain; a luxury hotel in Dubai was set ablaze.

Gulf rulers had spent the past two months trying to convince Donald Trump not to strike Iran, fearing exactly this sort of retaliation. Oman tried to mediate talks over a nuclear deal that would prevent war. Those efforts failed. They are desperate to avoid a prolonged war, which could shatter their reputation as safe hubs for business and tourism. Some firms are already drawing up plans in case they have to evacuate staff. Yet the harder Iran hits Gulf states, the more it may end up pushing them into alignment with America.

Sign up to enjoy a mind-expanding mix of stories, delivered six days a week

The war’s impact on energy prices

When Iran and Israel exchanged missiles last June, global oil prices jumped by 7%. The current conflict may have an even greater impact.

There are already reports that Iran’s Revolutionary Guard Corps are stopping ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz to the Persian Gulf. A complete closure of the waterway, through which 31% of all seaborne crude travels, could rapidly reduce supply worldwide. Meanwhile explosions have been heard on Kharg Island, Iran’s main oil-export terminal.

Analysts expect the price of a barrel to rise to $85-90 when markets reopen late on Sunday evening, up from $73 on Friday. Further disruption is possible. The Organisation of the Petroleum Exporting Countries may help matters by increasing production elsewhere; its members will meet on Sunday. But the longer the conflict drags on, the more likely it is to send energy markets reeling.

I’m glad you like adverbs — I adore them; they are the only qualifications I really much respect.

Henry James
